title: Dysophylla pumila
taxonomy: Dysophylla pumila is a species of flowering plant in the family Lamiaceae. It is native to Australia and is a small shrubby perennial herb. The classification of this plant places it in the order Lamiales. This order also includes other plants such as mint and basil. Dysophylla pumila is a relatively rare species and is not well-studied.
uses: Dysophylla pumila is a plant species used in traditional medicine, particularly in the Himalayas. It is reported to have various medicinal properties, including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects. The plant's extracts are believed to have potential therapeutic applications in treating certain health conditions. intro: Dysophylla pumila, also known as Korean mint or Korean mountain mint, is a perennial plant species belonging to the Lamiaceae family. This plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may aid in soothing digestive issues and alleviating symptoms of anxiety and stress. The bioactive constituents of Dysophylla pumila include iridoid glycosides, such as aucubin and harpagide, as well as flavonoids and phenolic acids, contributing to its medicinal properties. Dysophylla pumila is commonly used in traditional herbal preparations, including teas, infusions, and tinctures, to promote relaxation, ease digestive discomfort, and support overall well-being.
